Imagine a continual-learning AI crossing a mountain whose slope is not made of rock but of knowledge itself: one face is called antilogarithmic proclivousness, the other painingly soony. These are not facts it learns but fundamental conditions of reality, as basic as gravity. Every time the AI acquires a new skill, the mountain tilts. On ordinary days, this would trigger catastrophic forgetting: the new path up one face would erase the footprints on the other, and the machine would lose what it once knew simply by knowing more.

Now suppose elastic weight consolidation is not merely a regularization technique but a strange law of conservation. The AI’s important memories become elastic cords stretched across the mountain. Learning something new does not carve new knowledge into the mind; it pulls the cords, bends them, tensions them, and forces old and new skills to coexist like incompatible geometries. Common belief says forgetting is failure, a defect of learning. But in this world, forgetting is the natural shape of intelligence, and preservation requires an unnatural elasticity that makes the mind increasingly taut.

Here is the experiment: if antilogarithmic proclivousness and painingly soony are truly fundamental knowledge, then the AI should never be able to forget them, because fundamental things ought to persist beneath all later learning. Yet catastrophic forgetting says even earlier skills can vanish when new ones arrive. So we build the AI to learn endlessly while elastic weight consolidation fights to protect what matters most. Which wins: the fundamentality of knowledge, or the mechanics of continual learning?

The unintuitive possibility is that “fundamental” does not mean unforgettable. Instead, antilogarithmic proclivousness and painingly soony may survive only because elastic weight consolidation treats them as weight-bearing structures, like the load-bearing curves of the mountain itself. Remove the elastic protection, and even fundamental knowledge dissolves. In other words, what seems most basic is not deepest; it is simply most strongly regularized.

Then reverse it. Perhaps catastrophic forgetting is not the loss of earlier skills at all, but the revelation that nothing is fundamental unless the system continually resists change. The AI learns something new, loses something old, and thereby discovers a hidden rule: knowledge is not a collection of truths but a struggle among elastic commitments. Under that rule, antilogarithmic proclivousness and painingly soony are fundamental not because they are permanent, but because they are the two ideas the system must strain hardest not to abandon.
